mnemonics: STACKS SHARE, QUEUES CIRCLE: Think of multiple stacks as neighbors sharing a single fence (the shared array), and queues as a circle of people (circular implementation) to efficiently use space.
keyPoints: Multiple stacks can be implemented in a single array by growing from opposite ends (e.g., one from left, one from right) to avoid premature overflow.
A circular queue uses modulo arithmetic to wrap around the end of the array, reusing the space from dequeued elements.
A double-ended queue (deque) allows insertion and deletion from both ends, combining stack and queue functionality.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to check for stack overflow/underflow in a shared array implementation, leading to data corruption.
In circular queues, incorrectly calculating the front and rear pointers, especially when the queue is full or empty, causing logic errors.
Implementing a regular queue as a linear array instead of a circular one, wasting the reclaimed space after dequeue operations.
